answer:
Definition

A Todo App at intermediate level goes beyond basic CRUD — it incorporates drag-and-drop reordering, optimistic updates with rollback, real-time sync across tabs/devices, filtering/sorting with URL-persisted state, and batch operations (mark all complete, bulk delete). It tests your ability to manage complex client-side state while maintaining server consistency.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the manager's approval queue is essentially an advanced todo list: leave requests arrive in real-time via Socket.io, managers can bulk-approve/reject, filter by department or date range, and the queue updates optimistically. Reordering priorities uses the same drag-and-drop pattern as a reorderable todo list, backed by a Prisma sortOrder field.

Code Example

// Advanced Todo API — bulk operations + reorder (Fastify + Prisma)
fastify.post('/api/todos/bulk-update', { preHandler: [authenticate] }, async (req) => {
  const { ids, action } = req.body; // action: 'complete' | 'delete'

  if (action === 'complete') {
    await prisma.todo.updateMany({
      where: { id: { in: ids }, userId: req.user.id },
      data: { completed: true, completedAt: new Date() },
    });
  } else if (action === 'delete') {
    await prisma.todo.deleteMany({
      where: { id: { in: ids }, userId: req.user.id },
    });
  }

  // Broadcast to other tabs via Socket.io
  fastify.io.to(`user:${req.user.id}`).emit('todos:updated', { ids, action });
  return { success: true, affected: ids.length };
});

// Reorder with position tracking
fastify.patch('/api/todos/reorder', { preHandler: [authenticate] }, async (req) => {
  const { orderedIds } = req.body;
  const updates = orderedIds.map((id, index) =>
    prisma.todo.update({
      where: { id, userId: req.user.id },
      data: { sortOrder: index },
    })
  );
  await prisma.$transaction(updates);
  return { success: true };
});

// React hook for optimistic batch complete
function useBulkComplete(setTodos) {
  return async (ids) => {
    const snapshot = [...todos]; // save for rollback
    setTodos(prev => prev.map(t =>
      ids.includes(t.id) ? { ...t, completed: true } : t
    ));
    try {
      await api.post('/todos/bulk-update', { ids, action: 'complete' });
    } catch {
      setTodos(snapshot); // rollback on failure
      toast.error('Bulk update failed');
    }
  };
}

Debugging Narrative

In the HR Leave System, bulk-approving 15 leave requests at once caused a deadlock in PostgreSQL. The $transaction locked rows in unpredictable order, and two concurrent bulk approvals locked overlapping rows in opposite order. Manoj fixed it by sorting IDs before the transaction — ensuring all transactions lock rows in the same order, eliminating deadlocks. He also added a retry mechanism: if a deadlock error (code P2034) occurs, the operation retries up to 3 times with exponential backoff.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallImpactMitigation
Reorder sends N separate UPDATE queriesSlow on 100+ items, risk of partial updateUse $transaction to batch all updates atomically
Optimistic UI without snapshot for rollbackFailed API leaves UI in incorrect stateAlways save pre-mutation state; restore on catch
Socket.io emits to self causing double updateUI flickers or shows duplicate state changeEmit with broadcast flag so sender is excluded
No ownership check on bulk operationsUser can complete/delete another user's todosAlways include userId: req.user.id in WHERE clause

Validation Strategy

  • E2E: Playwright test — create 10 todos, select 5, bulk complete, assert only those 5 show as completed.
  • Concurrency: Two parallel bulk-update requests with overlapping IDs — assert no deadlock and consistent final state.
  • Real-time: Open two browser tabs, complete a todo in tab A, assert tab B updates within 500ms via Socket.io.
  • Rollback: Mock API failure on bulk delete — assert UI reverts to previous state with error toast.

Definition

Debugging a Todo App at intermediate level means diagnosing issues that arise from state synchronization, race conditions, memory leaks from subscriptions, and data inconsistencies between optimistic UI and server state. These bugs are subtle — the app "works" in happy-path testing but breaks under concurrent use, slow networks, or after prolonged use.

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, contact list operations mirror todo patterns: create, edit, delete, and reorder favorites. A persistent bug caused the contact list to show stale data after editing — the edit modal closed, but the list still showed the old phone number. The root cause was a missing cache invalidation after the PUT request; React Query's cache retained the old data because the mutation didn't trigger a refetch.

Code Example

// BUG: Stale list after editing a contact
// BEFORE (broken) — mutation succeeds but list shows old data
const editContact = async (id, data) => {
  await api.put(`/contacts/${id}`, data);
  setShowModal(false); // list still shows old name!
};

// AFTER (fixed) — invalidate React Query cache after mutation
import { useMutation, useQueryClient } from '@tanstack/react-query';

const queryClient = useQueryClient();
const mutation = useMutation({
  mutationFn: (data) => api.put(`/contacts/${data.id}`, data),
  onSuccess: () => {
    queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['contacts'] });
    setShowModal(false);
  },
});

// BUG: Race condition on rapid checkbox toggle
// BEFORE — two quick clicks send: PATCH completed=true, PATCH completed=false
const toggleTodo = async (id) => {
  const todo = todos.find(t => t.id === id);
  await api.patch(`/todos/${id}`, { completed: !todo.completed });
};

// AFTER — debounce + use latest state
const pendingToggles = useRef(new Map());
const toggleTodo = (id) => {
  const current = todos.find(t => t.id === id);
  const newVal = !current.completed;
  setTodos(prev => prev.map(t => t.id === id ? { ...t, completed: newVal } : t));

  clearTimeout(pendingToggles.current.get(id));
  pendingToggles.current.set(id, setTimeout(async () => {
    await api.patch(`/todos/${id}`, { completed: newVal });
    pendingToggles.current.delete(id);
  }, 300));
};

// BUG: Memory leak from Socket.io listener
// BEFORE — listener accumulates on every re-render
useEffect(() => {
  socket.on('todos:updated', handleUpdate); // no cleanup!
});

// AFTER — proper cleanup on unmount
useEffect(() => {
  socket.on('todos:updated', handleUpdate);
  return () => socket.off('todos:updated', handleUpdate);
}, []);

Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, after running the app for 30+ minutes, the browser tab's memory usage climbed from 80MB to 400MB. Manoj used Chrome DevTools' Memory tab to take heap snapshots — each snapshot showed thousands of detached DOM nodes. The culprit: a Socket.io event listener added inside useEffect without a cleanup function. Every time the ContactsList component re-rendered (on every filter change), a new listener was added without removing the old one. After 50 filter changes, 50 listeners were firing simultaneously, each creating DOM updates. Adding return () => socket.off(...) in the useEffect fixed the leak.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallSymptomDebug ToolFix
Stale cache after mutationList shows old data until manual refreshReact Query DevToolsqueryClient.invalidateQueries()
Race condition on rapid toggleCheckbox flickers and settles on wrong stateNetwork tab — see conflicting PATCHesDebounce + use latest local state
Memory leak from event listenerTab memory grows over time, page slows downChrome Memory profileruseEffect cleanup return
Missing error boundaryOne bad todo JSON crashes entire listConsole error + blank screenWrap list in ErrorBoundary

Validation Strategy

  • Stale data: Edit contact, assert list updates without manual refresh (Playwright).
  • Race condition: Programmatically toggle same checkbox 10 times in 500ms — assert final state matches server.
  • Memory leak: Mount/unmount component 100 times in test — assert no detached DOM nodes via performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory().
  • Error resilience: Inject malformed JSON in API response — assert ErrorBoundary renders fallback, not white screen.

Definition

An intermediate Auth Module goes beyond login/logout to include refresh-token rotation, role-based access control (RBAC), multi-device session management, account lockout after failed attempts, password reset flows, and OAuth integration. It handles token lifecycle, secure storage, and the interplay between authentication (who you are) and authorization (what you can do).

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, auth is layered: JWT access tokens (15-min expiry) + refresh tokens (7-day expiry, stored in httpOnly cookies). Three roles — Employee, Manager, Admin — each unlock different UI views and API endpoints. The refresh token rotates on every use (one-time use) to prevent token theft from granting permanent access. Socket.io connections also require token verification on the handshake.

Code Example

// Refresh token rotation (Fastify + Prisma + PostgreSQL)
fastify.post('/api/auth/refresh', async (req, reply) => {
  const { refreshToken } = req.cookies;
  if (!refreshToken) return reply.status(401).send({ error: 'No refresh token' });

  // Find the stored token
  const storedToken = await prisma.refreshToken.findUnique({
    where: { token: refreshToken },
    include: { user: true },
  });

  if (!storedToken || storedToken.revoked) {
    // Token reuse detected — revoke ALL user sessions
    if (storedToken) {
      await prisma.refreshToken.updateMany({
        where: { userId: storedToken.userId },
        data: { revoked: true },
      });
    }
    return reply.status(401).send({ error: 'Token reuse detected' });
  }

  if (storedToken.expiresAt < new Date()) {
    return reply.status(401).send({ error: 'Refresh token expired' });
  }

  // Rotate: revoke old, issue new pair
  await prisma.refreshToken.update({
    where: { id: storedToken.id },
    data: { revoked: true },
  });

  const newAccessToken = jwt.sign(
    { userId: storedToken.user.id, role: storedToken.user.role },
    process.env.JWT_SECRET,
    { expiresIn: '15m' }
  );

  const newRefreshToken = crypto.randomUUID();
  await prisma.refreshToken.create({
    data: {
      token: newRefreshToken,
      userId: storedToken.user.id,
      expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + 7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000),
    },
  });

  reply.setCookie('refreshToken', newRefreshToken, {
    httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'strict', path: '/api/auth',
  });

  return { accessToken: newAccessToken };
});

// RBAC middleware factory
function requireRole(...roles) {
  return (req, reply, done) => {
    if (!roles.includes(req.user.role)) {
      return reply.status(403).send({ error: 'Insufficient permissions' });
    }
    done();
  };
}

// Usage: only managers and admins can approve
fastify.patch('/api/leaves/:id/approve',
  { preHandler: [authenticate, requireRole('manager', 'admin')] },
  async (req) => {
    return prisma.leaveRequest.update({
      where: { id: req.params.id },
      data: { status: 'approved', approvedBy: req.user.userId },
    });
  }
);

Debugging Narrative

In the HR Leave System, managers reported being randomly logged out mid-session. Manoj traced it through the network tab: the refresh endpoint returned 401 "Token reuse detected." The cause was a race condition — when two API calls failed simultaneously (both got 401 for expired access token), both triggered the refresh endpoint concurrently. The first call rotated the refresh token, and the second call sent the now-revoked old token, triggering the security mechanism that revoked all sessions. Manoj fixed it by queuing refresh requests: a single Promise is created for the first refresh call, and all subsequent calls within a 1-second window await that same Promise instead of making their own refresh requests.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallImpactMitigation
Concurrent refresh requests cause token reuse detectionUser unexpectedly logged outQueue/deduplicate refresh calls with a shared Promise
Storing role only in JWT without server checkStale role after admin changes user's roleRe-verify role from DB on sensitive operations
No account lockout on brute-force attemptsPasswords crackable via automated login attemptsLock account after 5 failed attempts; require CAPTCHA
Refresh token stored in localStorageXSS can steal long-lived tokenhttpOnly secure cookie; never accessible to JS

Validation Strategy

  • Rotation test: Use refresh token → assert new pair issued → reuse old refresh token → assert ALL sessions revoked.
  • RBAC test: Employee calls /leaves/:id/approve → assert 403; Manager calls → assert 200.
  • Concurrency: Fire 5 simultaneous API calls with expired access token → assert only 1 refresh request sent.
  • Lockout: Send 6 wrong passwords → assert account locked and correct error message returned.

Definition

Debugging an Auth Module at intermediate level involves diagnosing token lifecycle issues (silent refresh failures, token reuse detection false positives), RBAC misconfigurations (role escalation, stale permissions), CORS/cookie problems (cookies not sent cross-origin), and session management bugs (ghost sessions, logout not revoking tokens). These bugs are dangerous because they often create security vulnerabilities.

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, deployed on Render, the auth cookies stopped working after adding a custom domain. The httpOnly cookie was set with sameSite: 'strict' and secure: true, but the frontend was served from contactshub.app while the API was on api.contactshub.app. The strict SameSite policy blocked the cookie from being sent cross-subdomain. Changing to sameSite: 'lax' and adding domain: '.contactshub.app' fixed it.

Code Example

// BUG: Infinite refresh loop
// BEFORE — expired refresh token causes endless retry
axios.interceptors.response.use(
  (res) => res,
  async (error) => {
    if (error.response?.status === 401) {
      await refreshToken(); // if refresh also 401s, this loops forever!
      return axios(error.config);
    }
    return Promise.reject(error);
  }
);

// AFTER — track refresh attempts, redirect to login on failure
let isRefreshing = false;
let failedQueue = [];

axios.interceptors.response.use(
  (res) => res,
  async (error) => {
    const originalRequest = error.config;

    // Don't retry the refresh endpoint itself
    if (originalRequest.url === '/api/auth/refresh') {
      logout(); // clear tokens, redirect to login
      return Promise.reject(error);
    }

    if (error.response?.status === 401 && !originalRequest._retry) {
      originalRequest._retry = true;

      if (isRefreshing) {
        // Queue this request until refresh completes
        return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
          failedQueue.push({ resolve, reject });
        }).then(() => axios(originalRequest));
      }

      isRefreshing = true;
      try {
        await axios.post('/api/auth/refresh');
        failedQueue.forEach(({ resolve }) => resolve());
        failedQueue = [];
        return axios(originalRequest);
      } catch {
        failedQueue.forEach(({ reject }) => reject(error));
        failedQueue = [];
        logout();
        return Promise.reject(error);
      } finally {
        isRefreshing = false;
      }
    }
    return Promise.reject(error);
  }
);

// BUG: Cookie not sent cross-subdomain
// BEFORE
reply.setCookie('refreshToken', token, {
  httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'strict', path: '/api/auth',
});
// AFTER — allow cross-subdomain + lax policy
reply.setCookie('refreshToken', token, {
  httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'lax',
  domain: '.contactshub.app', path: '/api/auth',
});

Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub deployed on Render, login worked perfectly in development (localhost) but failed in production. The login API returned 200 with a Set-Cookie header, but subsequent API calls didn't include the cookie. Manoj spent hours checking CORS and credentials — both were correct. The real issue was the sameSite: 'strict' attribute: it blocks cookies on cross-site navigation, and since the frontend was on a different subdomain, the browser treated it as cross-site. Changing to sameSite: 'lax' and setting domain: '.contactshub.app' made the cookie available across subdomains. He verified the fix by checking the Application > Cookies panel in DevTools.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallSymptomDebug MethodFix
Infinite refresh loopHundreds of /refresh requests in Network tabNetwork tab: filter by /refreshDon't retry the refresh endpoint; redirect to login
Cookie not sent cross-origin401 on every authenticated request in productionApplication > Cookies: check domain/pathSet domain to parent, sameSite: lax, credentials: include
JWT role stale after admin changeDemoted user retains admin access until token expiresDecode JWT (jwt.io) — check role claimRe-fetch role from DB on sensitive endpoints
Logout doesn't revoke refresh tokenStolen token usable after user logs outUse revoked token post-logout — assert 401Mark refresh token as revoked in DB on logout

Validation Strategy

  • Refresh loop: Mock refresh endpoint to return 401 — assert only 1 refresh attempt, then redirect to login.
  • Cookie cross-domain: Deploy to staging with subdomains — assert cookie present in DevTools Application tab.
  • Logout revocation: Logout → extract old refresh token → call /refresh with it → assert 401.
  • Playwright E2E: Login → wait 16 minutes (access token expires) → perform action → assert silent refresh succeeds.

Definition

An intermediate Pagination API goes beyond basic offset/cursor to handle complex filtering, multi-column sorting, search integration, response caching, and consistent results under concurrent writes. It addresses the real-world challenge of paginating over filtered, sorted, and constantly-changing datasets while maintaining performance on tables with millions of rows.

Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Dashboard, the deals table supports filtering by stage (Lead, Proposal, Won, Lost), date range, assigned salesperson, and deal value range — all combined with pagination. The API builds dynamic Prisma where clauses from query parameters and caches the total count per unique filter combination in Redis (5-minute TTL) to avoid expensive COUNT queries on every page navigation.

Code Example

// Advanced pagination with dynamic filters (Fastify + Prisma)
fastify.get('/api/deals', { preHandler: [authenticate] }, async (req) => {
  const {
    page = 1, pageSize = 20, stage, minValue, maxValue,
    assignee, sortBy = 'createdAt', sortOrder = 'desc',
    search,
  } = req.query;

  // Build dynamic WHERE clause
  const where = {
    ...(stage && { stage }),
    ...(assignee && { assigneeId: assignee }),
    ...(minValue || maxValue ? {
      value: {
        ...(minValue && { gte: parseFloat(minValue) }),
        ...(maxValue && { lte: parseFloat(maxValue) }),
      },
    } : {}),
    ...(search && {
      OR: [
        { title: { contains: search, mode: 'insensitive' } },
        { company: { contains: search, mode: 'insensitive' } },
      ],
    }),
  };

  // Cache key from filter params
  const cacheKey = `deals:count:${JSON.stringify(where)}`;
  let total = await redis.get(cacheKey);

  const [data, countResult] = await Promise.all([
    prisma.deal.findMany({
      where,
      skip: (parseInt(page) - 1) * parseInt(pageSize),
      take: Math.min(parseInt(pageSize), 100), // cap at 100
      orderBy: { [sortBy]: sortOrder },
    }),
    total ? Promise.resolve(null) : prisma.deal.count({ where }),
  ]);

  if (!total) {
    total = countResult;
    await redis.set(cacheKey, total, 'EX', 300); // 5-min cache
  }

  return {
    data,
    pagination: {
      page: parseInt(page),
      pageSize: parseInt(pageSize),
      total: parseInt(total),
      totalPages: Math.ceil(parseInt(total) / parseInt(pageSize)),
    },
  };
});

// Prisma migration — composite index for fast filtered pagination
// schema.prisma
// @@index([stage, value, createdAt])
// @@index([assigneeId, createdAt])

Debugging Narrative

In the CRM Dashboard, paginating deals filtered by "Won" stage was fast (200ms) but filtering by "Lead" stage took 4 seconds on page 1. Manoj checked the PostgreSQL query plan with EXPLAIN ANALYZE — the "Won" filter used the composite index (stage, createdAt) because the sort was on createdAt, but the "Lead" filter had 10x more rows and the planner chose a sequential scan instead. Adding SET enable_seqscan = off temporarily confirmed the index was faster. The real fix was adding WHERE stage = 'Lead' as a partial index: CREATE INDEX idx_deals_lead ON deals(createdAt) WHERE stage = 'Lead' — this reduced the query from 4s to 50ms.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallImpactMitigation
COUNT(*) on every page requestSlow on large filtered datasetsCache count in Redis per filter combination
No index on sort column with filterFull table scan on filtered paginationComposite index matching filter + sort columns
Client can request pageSize=99999OOM on server, massive JSON responseServer-side cap: Math.min(pageSize, 100)
Inconsistent results when data changes mid-paginationDuplicate or missing items across pagesUse cursor-based pagination for real-time data; offset for static reports

Validation Strategy

  • Performance: Seed 1M deals, paginate with filters — assert response < 200ms with proper indexes.
  • Filter accuracy: Filter by stage=Won, page through all results — assert every item has stage=Won.
  • Cache consistency: Add a deal, navigate to page 1 — assert count updates within 5 minutes (TTL).
  • Security: Send pageSize=10000 — assert server caps at 100.

Definition

Debugging a Pagination API at intermediate level tackles off-by-one errors in page calculations, performance degradation on deep pages, inconsistent results from concurrent writes, filter/sort interactions that bypass indexes, and cache invalidation issues. These bugs often surface only in production with real data volumes and concurrent users.

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, cursor-based pagination broke when two users had contacts with identical updatedAt timestamps. The cursor used updatedAt as the seek value, but when two contacts had the same timestamp, the cursor skipped one. The fix was switching to a compound cursor: updatedAt + id, ensuring uniqueness even when timestamps collide.

Code Example

// BUG: Cursor skips items with same timestamp
// BEFORE — cursor on updatedAt only
fastify.get('/api/contacts', async (req) => {
  const { cursor, limit = 20 } = req.query;
  return prisma.contact.findMany({
    where: {
      userId: req.user.id,
      ...(cursor && { updatedAt: { lt: new Date(cursor) } }), // SKIPS duplicates!
    },
    take: parseInt(limit),
    orderBy: { updatedAt: 'desc' },
  });
});

// AFTER — compound cursor: updatedAt + id
fastify.get('/api/contacts', async (req) => {
  const { cursorDate, cursorId, limit = 20 } = req.query;
  const where = {
    userId: req.user.id,
    ...(cursorDate && cursorId && {
      OR: [
        { updatedAt: { lt: new Date(cursorDate) } },
        {
          updatedAt: new Date(cursorDate),
          id: { lt: cursorId }, // break ties with ID
        },
      ],
    }),
  };

  const contacts = await prisma.contact.findMany({
    where,
    take: parseInt(limit) + 1,
    orderBy: [{ updatedAt: 'desc' }, { id: 'desc' }],
  });

  const hasMore = contacts.length > limit;
  const data = hasMore ? contacts.slice(0, -1) : contacts;
  const last = data[data.length - 1];

  return {
    data,
    hasMore,
    nextCursor: hasMore ? { date: last.updatedAt, id: last.id } : null,
  };
});

// BUG: Off-by-one — page 1 returns 21 items
// BEFORE
const contacts = await prisma.contact.findMany({
  skip: page * pageSize, // page 1 skips 20, starts at 21st
  take: pageSize,
});

// AFTER — subtract 1 from page for 1-indexed pagination
const contacts = await prisma.contact.findMany({
  skip: (page - 1) * pageSize, // page 1 skips 0, starts at 1st
  take: pageSize,
});

Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, a user reported "missing contacts" — they had 47 contacts but pagination only showed 45. Manoj wrote a test that fetched all pages and collected IDs into a Set. Two contacts with identical updatedAt timestamps (both imported in the same bulk operation) were skipped because the cursor was WHERE updatedAt < cursor_timestamp — the < operator skipped both when the cursor landed on their shared timestamp. The compound cursor (timestamp + id) ensured every contact appeared exactly once. He added an integration test that seeds contacts with duplicate timestamps and asserts all are returned across pages.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallSymptomDebug MethodFix
Single-column cursor on non-unique fieldItems skipped when values collideCollect all IDs across pages — check for gapsCompound cursor (timestamp + unique ID)
0-indexed vs 1-indexed page paramPage 1 shows wrong items or emptyCompare expected vs actual first itemStandardize: (page - 1) * pageSize
Deep OFFSET on large tablePage 500 takes 10+ secondsEXPLAIN ANALYZE shows sequential scanSwitch to cursor/keyset pagination
Cached count doesn't match filtered resultsPagination says 10 pages but page 10 is emptyCompare cached count vs fresh countInvalidate cache on insert/delete events

Validation Strategy

  • Completeness: Seed 100 contacts, paginate all pages — assert exactly 100 unique IDs collected.
  • Duplicate timestamps: Seed 5 contacts with identical updatedAt — assert all 5 appear across pages.
  • Off-by-one: Request page 1 with pageSize=10 — assert first item is the newest, 10 items returned.
  • Performance: Seed 500K contacts, request page 25000 (offset) vs cursor — assert cursor is 50x faster.
